---
title: "1. Introduction"
subtitle: "Geospatial Data Analysis"
author: "Prof. Dr. Halldór Janetzko"
header-logo: "_extensions/halldorjanetzko/hslu-reveal-theme-de/logos/hslu-logo.svg"
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-image: "figures/cartography_teaser.png"
    data-background-size: contain
    data-background-opacity: "0.8"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, brand, ./_extensions/halldorjanetzko/hslu-reveal-theme-de/hslu_theme.scss, local.scss ]
    chalkboard: true
    incremental: false
    slide-number: "c"
    hide-from-titleSlide: "all"
    embed-resources: false
    subtitle-as-header: true
    footer: "{{< meta title >}}"
    sc-sb-title: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    template-partials: 
      - toc-slide.html
    include-after-body: 
      - toc-add.html
      - clean_title_page.html
filters: 
  - reveal-header
slide-level: 3
---

## Today's menu {.unnumbered .unlisted}

```{=html}
<div id='toc'></div>
```


## Motivation

::: {.incremental}
- Who has ever used a Geographic Information System?
- Who has a GPS-enabled device (e.g., GPS mouse, smartphone GPS, car navigation system)?
- How often do you use it?
  - Daily
  - Weekly
  - Monthly
  - Occasionally
- Who has ever implemented something geo-related?
  - What was it?
:::

---

### Dr. Snow‘s Cholera Map

In the map made by **Dr. John Snow** in 1844, the death cases (indicated by bars) are clustered around the location of one of the water pumps. The cluster is encircled.  

![](figures/cholera-map-dr-john-snow-1844.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

::: {.tiny}
Cited and analysed by Gilbert and Tufte (Source: <http://johnsnow.matrix.msu.edu>)
:::

---


### First Law of Geography


::: callout-important
One of the things to remember from this class:
:::


> “Everything is related to everything else,  
> but near things are more related than distant things.”  
> — *Waldo Tobler, 1970*


## Cartography
### Definition

**Cartography**: the art, science, and technology of making maps,  
together with their study as documents and works of art,  
including maps, charts, sections, 3D models, and globes  
representing the Earth or other celestial bodies (after ICA, 1973).

---

### Development

- Origins in antiquity (oldest known records around 3600 BC in Mesopotamia)
- Ancient Greece: development of scientific cartography
- Roman Empire: application in military, administration, and trade
- 15th / 16th century: Earth and sea maps during the age of exploration
- 20th / 21st century: air and satellite imagery and computer usage (GIS)

---
